risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "patients were randomized to receive PtDA intervention or usual care using a 1:1 allocation ratio. Randomization took place ... using a centralized Internet randomization service (https://www.randomize.net); permuted blocks of 4 and 6 were used to balance treatment arms." Comment: This describes a centralized internet randomization service with permuted blocks, which indicates a proper random allocation sequence was used.

Quote: "Randomization took place prior to electrophysiology specialist consultations using a centralized Internet randomization service (https://www.randomize.net)... The use of https://www.randomize.net ensured that the allocation sequence was concealed from the research assistant." Comment: This explicitly describes a centralized web-based randomization service that concealed the allocation sequence from the recruiter, which prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment and meets low-risk criteria for allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, patients and the research assistant collecting data were not blinded to study group assignment." Comment: Participants and personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the main outcomes here (knowledge, decisional conflict, SURE test, preparedness) are plausibly influenced by awareness of allocation, so this domain is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention, patients and the research assistant collecting data were not blinded to study group assignment. The data analyst was blinded to group assignment." Comment: The outcome assessor was the research assistant collecting data, and they were explicitly not blinded. Key outcomes were largely patient-reported/subjective (knowledge questions, Decisional Conflict Scale, SURE test, Preparation for Decision-Making Scale), so knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. Although one outcome (3-month ICD implantation status) was obtained from the medical record, the main measured outcomes were susceptible to detection bias.

Quote: "The percentage of missing data was low (< 5%) and mainly restricted to the post-implantation follow-up surveys (where DCS follow-up and PDMS were not returned by mail and patients did not respond to requests)." Comment: Attrition/missing data were very limited overall and the paper indicates the main missingness was small and confined to mailed follow-up questionnaires, with no signal that it differed meaningfully by group or was related to the intervention/outcome. This is consistent with low risk of attrition bias.
